title: Versioning, Delete Markers, Object Lock, and Restore · Contract Lens: Worm Not Backup
summary: Predict unknown input, typed state, command, stream, promise, storage, cache, security, cost, and recovery outcomes before reviewing immutable-retention and recovery runbook; use version-specific branded identities, irreversible retention confirmation, recovery commands behind authorization, and executed restore evidence. Planned artifact: Add and verify the worm not backup increment in immutable-retention and recovery runbook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case…
objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, separate WORM retention, version recovery, replication, independent backup, restore testing, and account-compromise boundaries.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, apply governance, compliance, legal hold, retention date, and authorized bypass by version with irreversible-risk review.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Predict Worm Not Backup · Theory Worm Not Backup
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 2584 during a browser upload compatibility rollout. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to narrow versioning state and require version-specific retention and restore evidence; do not treat TypeScript types, a resolved promise, CORS, ETag, or an emulator as stronger proof than it is; then double concurrent Upload instances and preserve aggregate memory and worker budgets. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Worm Not Backup contract for the supplied data.
